data.title: Cyclist fatally hit by car in Hong Kong, the second in a week
data.description:
A cyclist has died in hospital after being knocked down by a car in Tai Po, a week after another fatal accident involving a bicycle in Hong Kong.
Police received a report of the accident at 7.25am on Monday.
The victim, a man in his sixties, was struck by the car on Ting Kok Road near Tai Po Industrial Estate.
He was rushed to Alice Ho Miu Ling Nethersole Hospital in Tai Po for treatment but was later pronounced dead.
The force subsequently arrested the driver for dangerous driving.

Last week, a 55-year-old female cyclist died after being hit by a van in Sheung Shui.
Dashcam footage circulating online showed the cyclist riding across a pedestrian crossing against a red light when she was struck on her right side by the black van.

data.title: Jensen Huang celebrates Lunar New Year with Nvidia employees in Shanghai
data.description:
Jensen Huang marked the coming Lunar New Year with Nvidia employees in Shanghai on Saturday, as the CEO of the world’s most valuable company made his first trip to China in 2026 amid uncertainties around its H200 graphics processing unit (GPU).
Huang received a rock-star welcome when he joined Nvidia’s annual Lunar New Year celebration, according to two employees who asked not to be named as they were not authorised to speak to the media. Huang gave a speech and handed out tangerines that he bought at a market earlier in the day, while staff members rushed to get his autograph, one employee said.
Huang is “celebrating Chinese New Year with our local employees in China”, an Nvidia spokesperson told the Post on Sunday, without elaborating on his agenda.
The latest move came as Beijing is expected to allow imports of the company’s H200, its second most powerful GPU, after temporarily holding the chips at the border.
The GPU, which remains an ideal option for Chinese big tech companies such as Alibaba Group Holding and ByteDance to train their artificial intelligence models, got Washington’s green light for exports earlier this month, on condition that the shipments not exceed 50 per cent of the volume sold domestically to US customers. Alibaba owns the Post.
The 62-year-old Huang began his latest trip to China earlier this week, after finishing his visit to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. He toured the chipmaker’s Shanghai office and met with staff to review Nvidia’s milestones and future product pipelines, Tencent Holdings’ news portal reported on Friday.
However, Huang did not mention the H200 to staff in Shanghai, according to the report.
Huang stopped at a grocery store in Shanghai’s busy Lujiazui area on Saturday, according to photos circulated on social media. He was seen shopping, tasting various fruits and looking relaxed.

Huang is next expected to visit Nvidia’s staff in Beijing and Shenzhen, continuing a routine he has followed over the past two years to mark the Lunar New Year with employees and business partners.
He was expected to arrive in Beijing on Monday, with the office’s annual Lunar New Year celebration taking place that evening, according to an employee.
Chinese authorities recently granted “in-principle” approval for tech giants including Alibaba, Tencent and ByteDance to purchase the H200 chips, Bloomberg reported on Friday. However, the firms were also encouraged to buy a certain amount of domestically produced chips, the report said.
With demand rising, some Chinese AI developers have turned to unofficial channels to secure the chips. One reseller said he had received “a couple of inquiries” earlier this week for the H200, adding that a server bundle containing eight H200 GPUs was selling for about 2.3 million yuan (US$330,403) – roughly 50 per cent above the official list price in mainland China, the Post previously reported.

data.title: Xiaomi’s SU7 becomes China’s first Tesla killer, outselling the Model 3Tesla’s grip on China’s premium sedan segment has loosened, as a bruising price war reshapes the EV landscape.
data.description:
After five years of playing catch-up, China has produced a worthy “Tesla killer”, a massive achievement for the country’s premium electric vehicle (EV) makers that is set to stoke national pride.
Sales of Xiaomi’s fully electric SU7 on the mainland stood at 258,164 last year, nearly 30 per cent higher than the Model 3’s deliveries of 200,361 units, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA).
Since its debut in China at the end of 2019, the Model 3, assembled at the Gigafactory 3 in Shanghai’s Lingang free-trade zone, led the country’s premium electric sedan category until 2024, despite massive efforts to unseat it by rivals, including Nio, Xpeng and IM Motors.
“Tesla’s dominance in the premium EV segment has been eroded by its Chinese competitors that are able to churn out vehicles on par with its technology standards while offering them at lower prices,” said Eric Han, a senior manager at Shanghai consultancy Suolei. “Xiaomi’s success is a strong boost for Chinese carmakers, which are all trying to move up the value chain.”

Tesla’s Shanghai factory also assembles the Model Y, which outsold all other SUVs – including petrol-powered rivals – on the mainland in 2025.
Chinese EV builders, led by BYD, the world’s largest EV maker, saw increased deliveries as their lower-priced cars proved popular with millions of middle and low-income drivers.
Only a handful of the country’s 50-odd EV makers are profitable due to heavy spending on research and development, with a vicious price war squeezing profit margins further.
Xiaomi, a smartphone vendor and EV start-up, began delivering its SU7 vehicles in March 2024. The popularity of its phones helped spur sales of the intelligent car featuring a preliminary autonomous driving system and a sophisticated digital cockpit.
The basic edition of the Model 3 is priced at 235,500 yuan, 9 per cent higher than the SU7.
In 2025, Tesla’s deliveries on the mainland fell 4.8 per cent from a year earlier to 625,698 units. The sales represented 4.8 per cent of the total EV sales nationwide, according to the CPCA.
Tesla’s market share in China topped 16 per cent in 2020 when the Shanghai factory began producing the Model 3. That dropped to 6.9 per cent in 2024 amid mounting com­petition from home-grown firms, which developed dozens of ­premium models with intelligent features similar to those in Tesla’s cars.
“Because the Model Y remains the bestselling SUV in China, it is too early to conclude Tesla cannot regain its lost ground,” said Gao Shen, an independent analyst in Shanghai, adding that domestic carmakers were either under pressure to break even or faced doubts regarding their cars’ safety and reliability.
In March, three people died in an accident involving an SU7 in Tongling, Anhui province, while the vehicle’s driver assistance system was turned on.
The crash prompted mainland authorities to tighten their oversight of early-stage autonomous driving technologies.
In October, another accident involving an SU7 Ultra model killed a 31-year-old man who was suspected of drink driving in Chengdu, Sichuan province. According to local media reports, passers-by tried to pull him out of the burning car, but were unable to open the doors.
Xiaomi founder and CEO Lei Jun said in November that the company would prioritise safety while designing and building new models.
Earlier this month, Xiaomi unveiled a refreshed version of the SU7, with a range of more than 900km on a single charge, to sustain its sales momentum.
Presales of the refreshed SU7 were launched at 229,900 yuan – 14,000 yuan, or 6.5 per cent, higher than the existing version.
The top edition offers a driving range of 902km, compared with 830km for the existing Pro variant.

data.title: Exclusive|Singapore Exchange hints at deeper ties with HKEX: can yuan business open doors?
data.description:
Singapore Exchange (SGX) Group is seeking to widen its cross-listing schemes with Shanghai and Shenzhen and explore partnerships with Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) and other exchanges, according to its CEO.
“Connectivity, in my view, is the new architecture for capital markets,” said Loh Boon Chye in an exclusive interview with the Post during his visit to Shenzhen last week. “We look towards more connectivity, not just with China but with other like-minded exchanges.”
Loh said if there was clear value, product innovation and widening of choices for investors in Hong Kong and Singapore, “why not a partnership?”
He said there was no concrete plan for how SGX might collaborate with HKEX, but he pointed to the yuan business as a potential area to explore. HKEX and SGX signed a memorandum of understanding on cooperation in developing the yuan business in December 2013, though progress since then had been limited.
“Singapore and Hong Kong are major international finance hubs in this part of the world,” Loh said. “HKEX and SGX are key platforms for the internationalisation of the yuan, and that may be an area where we can collaborate when the offshore yuan is traded by more international investors.”
Loh, who joined SGX as CEO in 2015, has been masterminding a number of partnerships. In November, SGX formed a partnership with Nasdaq to launch a global listing board in the middle of 2026 to allow companies to use one set of offering documents to seek listings and fundraising in both the US and Singapore. It also has partnerships with the stock exchanges of Thailand and Indonesia to use depository receipts for cross-listing.

Since 2022, SGX has established an exchange-traded fund (ETF) link with the Shenzhen Stock Exchange to allow cross-listing of ETFs, with the Shanghai Stock Exchange leg added a year later.
Loh spoke to the Post in Shenzhen on January 20 while attending the listing ceremony for the CSOP CSAM CSI A500 Index ETF, the 11th ETF trading under the China-Singapore ETF Link scheme.
“China has done well economically that its 2025 GDP [gross domestic product] achieved a 5 per cent growth rate, while Southeast Asia is still growing strongly,” he said. “The demand by investors for stocks or ETFs will grow, and there will be more cross-listings of ETFs in Singapore, Shenzhen and Shanghai.”
The ETF link now focuses on Chinese stocks, but Loh said he would like to see it expand to other Asian stocks and other asset classes such as fixed income or commodities.
Singapore is the third market worldwide with any form of connect scheme with China, after Hong Kong and London, though Hong Kong’s scale is much bigger as it has a wide range of cross-border trading of stocks, bonds, ETFs, swaps and wealth management products.
Loh said he did not see Hong Kong and Singapore as competitors, as each bourse had its own position and niche.

“HKEX and SGX are complementary to each other,” he said. “HKEX serves as a gateway to China, while SGX connects Asia to the world by providing global investors with Southeast Asian investment opportunities.”
Loh added that China and Asia had both expanded strongly over the past decade, with the region’s share of global GDP rising, a trend that supported capital market activity.
SGX has developed a strong position as a multi-asset exchange that has currency, fixed income, commodities and derivatives, but its stock market and initial public offering (IPO) pipeline are smaller than Hong Kong. HKEX’s main board was the world’s largest IPO market last year, with 114 companies raising US$37.22 billion, up 230 per cent from 2024.
SGX’s mainboard and Catalist recorded 16 IPOs in 2025, raising US$2.5 billion, a big jump from 2024 when it only had four deals totalling US$34.2 million. The rebound was driven by its biggest IPO in eight years in July, when NTT DC Reit raised US$773 million.
The IPO helped Singapore return to the league table to rank 14 in 2025, according to data from the London Stock Exchange Group.
“With NTT leading the way, other issuers are picking up the pace,” Loh said. “Asia and Singapore have both grown reasonably strongly and robustly. Interest rates globally have stabilised or are easing. The market sentiment has clearly improved across the region, which helps attract more companies to raise funds.”
While many Indian and Indonesian companies were raising funds in their domestic markets nowadays, Loh said they might opt for listings in Singapore or Hong Kong in future. “There are some companies that are very much geared towards the domestic economy, and they would first list in their home markets,” he said. “But when the companies want to expand overseas, they may go to SGX or HKEX to tap funds from international investors.”
Despite Nasdaq and London considering extending their trading hours to almost around the clock, Loh said he had no such plan for SGX. “Today, our currency, commodities and derivatives markets have been trading 22 hours a day,” he said. “I do not see a strong demand for extending the trading hours for the stock market.”
Singapore’s stock market operates from 9am to 5pm, with a one-hour midday break.

data.title: Chinese carmaker BYD chases 24% export growth with new models, more showrooms
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle (EV) king BYD aims for a 24 per cent increase in its overseas deliveries in 2026, buoyed by an expanded sales network and new model launches, as it pursues growth abroad to counteract a slowing domestic market.
The Shenzhen-based company, the world’s largest builder of pure electric and plug-in hybrid vehicles, expected to sell 1.3 million cars this year outside mainland China, up 24.3 per cent from 2025, said Li Yunfei, general manager of branding and public relations, in a media briefing on Saturday.
“We will launch more new models in some lucrative markets, which will include our Denza-branded vehicles,” he said. “Our network of dealers will be further expanded.”
Admitting that BYD faced challenges in driving up its sales both at home and abroad this year, Li said increasing brand awareness across the globe would provide a catalyst for international expansion.
Last year, BYD’s sales outside the mainland accounted for about 23 per cent of its total deliveries, which rose 7.7 per cent to 4.6 million units. Its proportion of overseas sales more than doubled from just 10 per cent in 2024.
BYD builds vehicles under five brands: mass-market brands Dynasty and Ocean, premium brand Denza, off-road-capable SUV brand Fang Cheng Bao and luxury brand Yangwang.
Its overseas distribution network now numbers 2,000 dealers.
The company planned to double showrooms in Europe to 2,000 in 2026 and pledged to create a complete local supply chain for its European production, said Stella Li, a BYD executive vice-president, on the sidelines of the IAA Mobility conference in Munich in September.
Li Yunfei said the company’s manufacturing facilities outside the mainland would ramp up production this year to cater to rising demand. Its factory in Hungary, with an annual capacity of 150,000 units, would start ­production in either March or April, he added. BYD also operates factories in Thailand and Brazil.
Deutsche Bank estimated mainland sales of passenger cars would fall by 5 per cent in 2026, while UBS predicted a 2 per cent decline because of the automotive industry’s overcapacity woes and softening government support.

EV buyers, who were exempt from a 10 per cent purchase tax last year, are now subject to a 5 per cent tax as Beijing gradually phases out the tax incentive. In 2028, the purchase tax will return to the normal 10 per cent.
On the mainland, the average net per-vehicle ­margin – the gap between the ­selling price and production costs such as raw materials, labour and logistics – stood at about 5,000 yuan (US$717) among Chinese carmakers, according to Nick Lai, head of auto research in Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan.
He said in October that this margin could rise fourfold to 20,000 yuan if carmakers were to export more vehicles to overseas markets, where their products could command higher prices.
“More Chinese carmakers, particularly EV builders, will up their ante on overseas markets to shore up profitability,” said Gao Shen, an independent analyst in Shanghai. “It will be interesting to see which Chinese-made cars will be more popular in the international markets.”
In December, Xu Jun, chief operating officer of Stellantis-backed Chinese EV maker Leapmotor, said the company predicted its overseas deliveries would represent 10 per cent of its total in 2026, compared with 5 per cent in 2025.

data.title: IMF raises China’s 2026 growth forecast to 4.5%, citing US ‘truce’ and stimulus roll-out
data.description:
The International Monetary Fund has raised its 2026 economic growth forecasts for both China and the United States by 0.3 percentage points, the latest sign that the pause in their trade war has eased pressure on both economies.
In its latest World Economic Outlook, the IMF’s flagship publication released on Monday, the Washington-based fund also upgraded its global economic growth estimate by 0.2 percentage points over its October forecast to 3.3 per cent this year.
China’s economy is now forecast to expand by 4.5 per cent in 2026, supported by lower US tariff rates and domestic stimulus measures. That projection, up 0.3 percentage points from the IMF’s October forecast, still remains below the 5 per cent growth rate in 2025 announced by Beijing’s National Bureau of Statistics earlier on Monday.
The IMF said the improved outlook reflects “lower US effective tariff rates on Chinese goods as a result of the year-long [US-China] trade truce agreed to in November and stimulus measures that are assumed to be implemented over two years”.
The fund also revised its projection for the US, estimating that the world’s largest economy would grow by 2.4 per cent in 2026 – an increase from its October forecast of 2.1 per cent – citing fiscal support, lower interest rates and the waning impact of higher trade barriers.
After several rounds of negotiations in 2025, Beijing and Washington reached an agreement in November that scaled back US President Donald Trump’s import tariffs, which had broken the 100 per cent mark in April. China agreed to ease export controls on rare earth metals, which are critical to hi-tech and automotive manufacturing.
On the stimulus front, Beijing has rolled out a series of measures since 2024. In December 2025, officials announced a package to boost household spending – long a weak spot in the economy – by expanding access to credit and encouraging new forms of consumption.
China’s central bank also cut policy relending rates by 0.25 percentage points on Thursday, with support directed towards private, technology and agricultural businesses.


The efforts came as fixed-asset investment – the country’s old growth driver – fell last year, while consumption had not been strong enough to offset a broader slowdown.
China’s record merchandise trade surplus, which totalled US$1.19 trillion in 2025, could also increase the likelihood of trade frictions this year.
Analysts from Standard Chartered wrote in a note last week that China would likely set a growth target of between 4.5 per cent and 5 per cent for 2026. Other analysts expect policymakers to maintain last year’s policy tone through 2026, saying Beijing could withstand a slightly lower growth target.
Goldman Sachs Research forecasted that China’s real gross domestic product would grow by 4.8 per cent this year, while Bank of America chief Greater China economist Helen Qiao told a forum at the University of Hong Kong on Sunday that Beijing could still reach 5 per cent – but only with sustained effort.
“It’s not a piece of cake where you could get it by relaxing, not trying hard and lying flat,” Qiao said.
Looking ahead, the IMF projects China’s growth to slow to 4.0 per cent in 2027, below the average for “emerging and developing Asia”.
The world’s second-largest economy could grow more slowly this year than last as credit growth lags, driven by “overcapacity” and government campaigns against cutthroat pricing, said Liang Yan, an economics professor at Willamette University in the US state of Oregon.
Manufacturers, a backbone of the economy, would likely borrow less money under these pressures, she said, adding that soft credit growth “indicates that there might be weaker economic activity”.

data.title: PLA urged to find a way to operate effectively under the gaze of US and Japan
data.description:
People’s Liberation Army activities in the Taiwan Strait are being closely watched by the United States and Japan and it needs to find a way to operate effectively while being surveilled, according to analysts.
A report by Beijing-based defence think tank Lande said the PLA’s most recent large-scale drills around Taiwan – dubbed Justice Mission 2025 – were launched with no warning yet the response from the US and Japan was immediate.
It said that highlighted the constant presence in the region of the two militaries and their ability to react quickly.
“Multi-dimensional surveillance” was also pointed out by other analysts, who said the PLA must factor this into planning if its goal was to carry out a surprise attack across the strait.
The think tank report said that when the two-day PLA exercise began on December 29, US and Japanese spy planes had either diverted from routine patrol routes to circle nearby or they carried out sorties to the drill areas.


The aircraft included the US MQ-4C Triton drone and Japanese surveillance plane the Falcon 2000MSA, according to the report, which cited open-source flight path tracking data.
As the surveillance aircraft approached, there was a surge in sensor data being transmitted by the planes in a short space of time, the report said, without giving a source.
“The entire process was not an ad hoc decision, but rather a well-established emergency response protocol was likely activated,” it concluded.
According to the think tank, the PLA must move away from a mindset of trying to avoid detection and recognise that any major deployment would be discovered given the extent of US and Japanese reconnaissance in the area.
“The PLA’s operational planning must pivot from an emphasis on concealing deployments and operational signatures to guaranteeing mission effectiveness,” the report said.
That could be done through “efficient manoeuvring, comprehensive information control, and systemic resilience”.
It said advanced US radar such as the AN/ZPY-3 on the MQ-4C drone and military datalinks could be countered by using an integrated electronic warfare system to “scan, control, jam and deceive”.
The report also suggested the “controlled release” of information to actively mislead enemy intelligence, effectively shaping what the adversary sees.
Other military analysts have also suggested the PLA make efforts to counter surveillance, including electronic jamming and intercepting sonobuoys – sonar devices dropped from aircraft for anti-submarine warfare
Song Zhongping, a military analyst and former PLA instructor, said a key challenge for the PLA was how to tackle the extensive US data transmission networks across the Pacific.
He said while the Kadena Air Base in Okinawa, Japan was a critical “intelligence hub” for the US in the region, its intelligence and data transmission was done mainly via satellite rather than a single location.
“So they can route data to Guam, or even back to the US mainland,” Song said.
The think tank report noted that Kadena and also Naha, a Japanese air base on Okinawa, were vulnerable to strikes.


Yue Gang, a military commentator and retired PLA colonel, said the US and Japanese response to last month’s drills would have been part of a contingency protocol.
But he said during an extended exercise – or even a real battle – the US could rapidly deploy additional military assets for surveillance, reducing its reliance on any single weapon system or base.
“After a day or two, they could mobilise their full range of resources to form a far more comprehensive reconnaissance network,” he said.
Yue noted that as the situation escalated, data collected in Okinawa could be transmitted to the home port of the US Navy’s Seventh Fleet in Yokosuka, Japan, or even to the US Indo-Pacific Command in Honolulu.
He said the PLA could “create obstacles” for intelligence gathering, including through the element of surprise.
Yue added that any PLA operation to counter reconnaissance activities – such as striking US satellites or facilities – would depend on the scale of the conflict.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. It has ramped up pressure on Taiwan since pro-independence leader William Lai Ching-te took office in 2024.
Most countries, including the US and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. However, Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is legally bound to supply Taiwan with weapons to defend itself.
Last month’s PLA drills were conducted soon after the US enraged Beijing when it approved an US$11.1 billion arms sale to Taiwan – the biggest ever.
It also came after ties between Beijing and Tokyo rapidly deteriorated following Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s suggestion in November that Japan could justify military intervention if there was a conflict over Taiwan.

data.title: Trump abandons ‘peace’ focus after Nobel snub in message to Norway PM: ‘World is not secure’
data.description:
US President Donald Trump told Norway’s prime minister he no longer needed to think “purely of peace” after failing to win the Nobel Peace Prize, in a message published on Monday.
Trump went on to reiterate his desire for the United States to take control of Greenland, an autonomous Danish territory, in the interest of world peace.
“Considering your Country decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS, I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace,” Trump said in a message to Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Store.
He said although peace would still be “predominant”, he could “now think about what is good and proper for the United States of America”.

Store’s office confirmed the authenticity of the document in an email to Agence France-Presse.
In a written comment, Store underlined that the Nobel Peace Prize was not awarded by the Norwegian government.
“I have clearly explained, including to President Trump, what is well known – the prize is awarded by an independent Nobel Committee,” Store said.
Trump also reiterated his desire for control of Greenland, which has sent jitters worldwide, in his message to the Norwegian premier.
“Denmark cannot protect that land from Russia or China,” Trump said.
“Why do they have a ‘right of ownership’ anyway? There are no written documents, it’s only that a boat landed there hundreds of years ago, but we had boats landing there, also,” he said.

“The World is not secure unless we have Complete and Total Control of Greenland,” he added.
Trump had campaigned hard to win last year’s Nobel Peace Prize for what he says are his efforts to stop eight wars.
Instead it went to Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado, who appeared in Oslo last month to collect her prize – following a daring escape from Venezuela by boat.
She dedicated the prize to Trump and last week gave her medal to him.

data.title: Goldman Sachs, Prada among top financial, luxury brands to join Hong Kong AFF
data.description:
The 19th Asian Financial Forum (AFF) kicks off in Hong Kong next week, highlighting the city’s strategic role in facilitating cross-border investment.
The forum would focus on helping mainland Chinese start-ups go global through Hong Kong initial public offerings (IPOs), while providing international firms with a gateway to the mainland, HSBC Hong Kong CEO Maggie Ng said on Monday.
Titled “Co-creating New Horizons amid an Evolving Landscape”, the annual government flagship event will run from January 26 to 27 at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai.
Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB) president Zou Jiayi, Morgan Stanley Asia CEO Gokul Laroia, Aberdeen Group chairman Douglas Flint, and Goldman Sachs Asia-Pacific (ex-Japan) president Kevin Sneader will be among the financial heavyweights joining the event, which is expected to be attended by 3,600 participants from 60 countries.
Besides financial players, luxury fashion brand Prada Group’s deputy chairman Paolo Zannoni and Hermès International retail culture and international relations director Bertrand Michaud will speak at the first Global Business Summit to discuss industry development, according to the programme unveiled on Monday.

The AFF would help promote the city as a “superconnector” and “super value-adder”, said Ng, who is also AFF’s steering committee chairwoman. “Many mainland enterprises have already begun their global expansion,” she said in a media briefing. “By the end of 2024, Chinese investors had established over 52,000 overseas companies worldwide, and the internationalisation trend will continue.”
“Faced with the new international economic and trade landscape, and the reshaping of industrial and supply chains, these companies have a strong desire to ‘go global’. Hong Kong can play its ‘superconnector’ role, providing a springboard to help them meet international standards, manage risks and seize new opportunities.”
Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu said the AFF would highlight the fact that the strong capital market in the city could help many mainland start-ups raise funds.
“The IPO market of Hong Kong ranked at the top of the world last year by raising over HK$280 billion (US$36 billion). The trend will continue this year with over 300 companies filing to list in the city,” Hui said.

Many potential start-up founders and executives will attend AFF to meet potential investors and other business partners from a wide range of sectors, including the electric vehicle (EV) industry, new energy and biotech companies, as well as global banks and investors.
There will also be sessions focused on family offices, wealth management and other investment issues.
“They are not just coming here to act as speakers, but they have a chance to come here to make deals,” Hui said, adding that the inaugural Global Industry Summit would allow different industry players to connect.
Gold will be a highlighted discussion at the two-day event. Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po said in his blog on Sunday that the city and the Shanghai Gold Exchange would sign a memorandum of understanding at AFF to pave the way for greater connectivity between the two markets.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, in his policy address in September, unveiled plans to support the city’s players in introducing more gold products, including tokenised gold and gold funds, as well as exploring cross-border gold trading between Hong Kong and Shanghai.

data.title: 11 dead, over 60 missing after Pakistan shopping centre fire smoulders for 24 hours
data.description:
Firefighters in Karachi began searching on ‌Monday for more than 60 missing people after a massive fire destroyed a shopping centre in Pakistan’s most populous city, while the death toll from the disaster rose to 11.
The fire started late on Saturday at the sprawling, multi-storey Gul Plaza in the city’s business district and ⁠burned for more than a day, hampering rescue efforts in the densely packed area.
After more than 24 hours of firefighting, crews moved to cooling and debris clearing on Sunday, although concerns grew that more victims could still be trapped inside.
Police surgeon Summaiya Syed said the death toll had risen to 11, while Karachi Mayor Murtaza Wahab said on Sunday that more than 60 people were still missing.
Firefighters said the lack of ventilation in the building, which houses more than 1,200 shops, caused thick smoke to fill the mall and slowed efforts to reach people trapped inside.

“It appears to have been caused by a circuit breaker,” Sindh province police chief Javed Alam Odho told reporters at the site on Sunday, adding that the layout of the market and the presence of flammable materials such as carpets and blankets caused parts of the fire to continue smouldering.
By Sunday evening, large sections of the building had collapsed, leaving twisted metal and debris strewn across the street, along with fallen air-conditioning units and shop signboards. Rescuers warned that the remaining structure was unstable and could collapse further.
According to rescue services, authorities received the first emergency call at 10.38pm on Saturday, reporting that ground-floor shops were on fire. By the time firefighters arrived, the flames had already spread to the upper floors, engulfing much of the building.
Shopkeepers and residents told local media that a delayed response and shortages of water and equipment had hampered early firefighting efforts, fuelling anger among traders who said decades of livelihoods had been wiped out.
Karachi, the capital of Sindh province, has a history of deadly fires, often blamed on poor safety standards and illegal construction. In November 2023, a fire at a shopping centre in the city killed 10 people and injured 22 others.
A massive fire at a garments factory in Karachi in 2012 killed 260 people.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Chinese team restores legendary Tang dynasty ‘golden’ armour found in Tibetan tomb
data.description:
“We will not leave the desert till we beat the foe, although in war our golden armour be outworn 100 times.”
Wang Changling’s famous poem from China’s Tang dynasty depicts soldiers in golden armour battling on the desert frontiers. However, for a long time, no Tang dynasty gold-plated armour was unearthed, leaving its appearance to the imagination.
But on January 14, the Key Laboratory of Archaeological Sciences and Cultural Heritage at the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences (CASS) unveiled the only known physical example of Tang dynasty “golden” armour – a meticulously restored suit of gilded bronze armour found in a royal tomb on the Tibetan plateau.

The restoration team behind the project not only rebuilt the artefact piece by piece but also produced a video reconstruction showing the likely appearance of the original gold armour.
The artefacts were salvaged and restored from the Tuyuhun royal tombs between 2022 and 2025, including armour, lacquerware and metal objects.
“We adopted a strategy of ‘disassembling the whole into parts and reassembling the parts into a whole’, conducting layered cleaning, extraction and protection while meticulously cataloguing each armour plate,” cultural heritage conservation expert Guo Zhengchen told a press conference at the armour unveiling.
The king’s tomb had been hit by looting and salvage excavations, leaving the armour plates in poor condition.
A 2024 excavation report in the journal Archaeology, titled “Excavation of Tomb No 1 at the Hot Water Cemetery in Dulan County, Qinghai, 2018,” describes the find: “Several bronze armour plates were discovered … The plates are nearly rectangular with a semicircular lower edge, measuring 10cm long, 5cm wide, and 0.3cm thick. A large number of lacquered armour pieces were also found”.
The copper and lacquered armour fragments were found intermixed and piled, with no clear structural relationship. They were fragile, at risk of shattering at the slightest touch.
“We recorded the original spatial information of each plate via 3D scanning and analysed their manufacturing techniques and composition using scanning electron microscopy and ultra-depth microscopy,” the conservators said.
Their analysis revealed that this was not merely bronze armour but gold armour with additional gilding. Based on this finding, they created a detailed restoration video.
The reconstructed armour in the video brings to life another Tang poem: “Like golden scale on water sunrays strike our coats of mail”.
Based on gold artefacts and silk textiles in the tomb and tree-ring dating of its wooden structure, the burial was dated to the mid-8th century – nearly 1,300 years ago – the conservation report stated. It said the tomb’s occupant was confirmed to be a Tuyuhun king and the gold armour displayed was likely among his most prized possessions.

The Tuyuhun kingdom was subjugated successively by the Sui and Tang dynasties. It later became a vassal of the Tubo empire based on the Tibetan plateau. In addition to the gold armour, the tomb yielded a large quantity of equestrian equipment and iron and lacquered armour.
This aligns with records in the New Book of Tang, compiled by 11th century historian Ouyang Xiu, which said the Tubo possessed “excellent armour covering the entire body, with only openings for the eyes, so that strong bows and sharp blades could not inflict serious harm”.
Dulan county, where the tomb was discovered, was an important transit point for east-west trade. There, the Qinghai Route was a vital artery of the Silk Road, allowing trade caravans from the Tubo empire and the Tang dynasty to travel east to Changan (modern Xian) and west to Xinjiang, Persia (modern Iran) and various western regions.

data.title: Meet Neville Sarony, the Hong Kong barrister who also writes, acts and sings
data.description:
I GREW UP IN A theatrical family. My mother was also on the stage and had just begun to really make a name for herself when she married my father (Leslie Sarony). Then we saw a little bit of his true character because he said, “There’s only one star in this family,” and she was told she couldn’t work any more. We used to go to wherever my father was playing summer seasons. He was half of The Two Leslies, which was the partnership that he began in, I think, 1937. I grew up watching them on stage. My father began (in vaudeville) before the first world war. He told me he first realised he could write songs during the war. So, the upshot is, I think, nearly 600 songs of his. And my brothers and I still get the royalties, which is rather fun.
THERE ARE THREE of us, all boys, I’m the eldest. There was not much distance between my birth and the outbreak of the second world war. We were living in London. My father had just bought this house and he decided it was dangerous for us to live there. So, he moved us to Yorkshire, to Huddersfield, where we lived for the next five years. After the war, we went back to London. At the age of 13, I went to a boarding school in Kent called Sutton Valence. It was 1952, things were coming off rationing. Before I left school, I was thinking of becoming an actor (but later decided to study law).

TWO WEEKS AFTER I left school I was in the army doing National Service. I joined the Queen’s Own Royal West Kent Regiment (which existed until 1961) and I was based in Maidstone. I took the War Office Selection Board (a series of tests to identify officer material) and then I was sent to the Mons Officer Cadet School (in Aldershot in Hampshire). I was commissioned out of Mons into the 7th Gurkha Rifles and sent to (then) Malaya, for arguably the two best years of my life. I was introduced to a culture that became part of my life. I was the battalion intelligence officer. The first thing I had to do was learn the language, Gorkhali – a mix of Nepalese and Urdu – otherwise no one speaks to you. It was quite an intense learning period, but I loved it. And then some Malay. The (Malayan) Emergency (also known as the Anti-British National Liberation War) was still on and we were hunting terrorists in the jungle.
IT WAS SO TEMPTING (to stay with the Gurkhas) but I had been offered a place at LSE (the London School of Economics) to read law and I had from an early age wanted to become a barrister. In my last year at LSE I got married. My wife was Nepali. She was the daughter of a Gurkha officer in our 1st Battalion, one of the first Gurkha officers to be given the Queen’s Commission and we met at Buckingham Palace. The queen was presenting a new pipe banner to the 6th Gurkhas (6th Queen Elizabeth’s Own Gurkha Rifles) and all brigade officers were invited to attend. We went along and as the daughter of a serving officer, she was invited. We married in 1963 and I graduated in 1964. I spent two years in London; it was terrible. It was very difficult to start at the bar and solicitors were notorious for paying late. So, we went to Nepal for three years and I wrote about that in my book Counsel in the Clouds (2014). I was the first foreign lawyer to practise in Nepal. I discovered that very few people were doing corporate law and I really focused on that. I became an adviser to members of the royal family. My wife, Bimala, was very creative and she set up the first boutique where she was designing clothes based on traditional Nepali styles but giving them a sort of Western twist.

AFTER THREE YEARS we returned to London. Bim opened a boutique in St Christopher’s Place, just off Oxford Street. It was called the Third Eye Boutique. In 1971, our daughter, Tanya, was born. Bim died in 1976 and that was a terrible time. Tanya was five and we took her mother’s ashes to her home in the Himalayas. I had to somehow look after Tanya and make a living. I married one of Bim’s sisters, which was probably a mistake. She wouldn’t live in the house, so we bought an almost derelict house in Chelsea and practically rebuilt it. (They divorced in 2000).
I DECIDED I DIDN’T LIKE the way the legal profession was going in England and I was thinking about America, but out of the blue a friend called from Hong Kong saying he was setting up chambers there. So, we came to Hong Kong in 1985. Tanya went to the Swiss German International School. I like doing both criminal and civil cases, which is not very common.

THE CASE WHICH probably excited the most emotion was the Shek Kip Mei one (in 1994), where 12 people died in a fire at a branch of HSBC and (my client) was charged with all 12 murders. He was an ordinary father of two daughters and then his wife disappeared. He spent three days looking for her and then went to the bank where he believed a friend (of his wife’s) worked and demanded to know where she was. He held a can of paint thinner and a lighter and told everyone to get out of the bank. While he was standing there talking, a police officer walked in and shouted at the man, and the lighter ignited the vapour from the can, which set fire to him and to the bank. HSBC had blocked up the rear exits of the bank so the only way out was through the front, which was now on fire. (He was given a 20-year jail sentence for manslaughter.)
(LAST SUMMER) we did an inquest where the jury returned an unlawful killing verdict of the father of a family we were representing. (In 2012) a taxi driver was arrested by the police and they hauled him up by his neck, broke his spine and made him paraplegic. He died 30 days later. The first inquest was held in 2018 and the jury returned a verdict of unlawful killing. Then the officer did a judicial review of the verdict, arguing that the coroner had misdirected the jury. So, a second inquest was directed and that jury also returned a verdict of unlawful killing. It was, in some ways, the most emotional outcome to a verdict that I’ve experienced.

I HAVE WRITTEN three novels, which are all based on a character called Max Devlin, who is an ex-Gurkha officer. Another one I’m working on is a book of legal anecdotes, which (my wife), Angel (Lau Wing-yi), is editing. It’s really a series of things which happened mainly to me, sometimes to others. Actually, Angel was a journalist. She’d done a criminology degree at Edinburgh. And then she came to the bar. She was introduced to me as a pupil and then things basically developed. I suppose it’s the longest period of engagement you could think of; it took me 16 years to actually pop the question, but we eventually got around to it.
IN DECEMBER 1997, I was preparing to leave Hong Kong. I was staying at a hotel in Kowloon and I looked out of my window and I saw this sign on the back of a building – it said “traditional jazz” and it was Ned Kelly’s Last Stand in Tsim Sha Tsui (where Sarony now sings regularly, having relocated to London only briefly in 1997). In 2004, I was approached by the Faust Festival to audition for this play, The Dresser, by Ronald Harwood. It’s a super part. The whole play is built around the relationship between the dresser and the actor-manager, who is a sort of old-fashioned actor-manager taking his own touring company around, putting on Shakespeare mainly. So the manager is on stage the whole time, he never leaves. My dressing table faced out three feet from the audience and I would make up my face into a mirror that wasn’t there. It was fascinating. The director (Jessica Lefkow) was clever.

THE PIANO IS, I think, my sort of go-to place when things are bad, I can go and play them out. I get a huge amount of satisfaction from music. I think one of the greatest joys for me is singing with good musicians. And that’s so much fun. Jason Cheng runs a big IT company but he moonlights as a great jazz pianist and he and I, we work together (Sarony singing). We have a sort of symbiotic relationship. We can literally do anything.
I DIVIDE MY time between Javea, in Valencia, Spain, and Hong Kong – Hong Kong is work and in Spain I’m writing a book of legal anecdotes drawn from things that happened in my career.

data.title: China’s demographic alarms blare as births hit historic low and population shrinks again
data.description:
China’s birth count plummeted to a record low last year, falling by about 10 million from its 2016 peak and slashing the total by more than half in less than a decade, as the country’s population shrank for a fourth consecutive year.
Only 7.92 million babies were born in 2025, down 17 per cent from 9.54 million in 2024, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics on Monday. This marked the lowest birth figure since records began in 1949 and broke the previous record low set in 2023.
China’s total population also fell by 3.39 million in 2025 to 1.4049 billion from 1.4083 billion a year earlier, the bureau said. By sheer numbers, that marked the steepest annual population decline on record, apart from during China’s devastating famine from 1959 to 1961.
Meanwhile, about 11.31 million people died last year – one of the highest totals in five decades.
“The pace of the decline is striking, particularly in the absence of major shocks,” said Su Yue, principal economist for China at the Economist Intelligence Unit.
She added that a reluctance among young people to get married, along with rising economic pressures – particularly an increase for women in the perceived cost of stepping away from employment – served as major birth deterrents.
“The data should serve as a strong signal for policymakers to place greater emphasis on domestic structural reforms,” she noted, calling for a more forceful policy response on fertility in the face of a shrinking population and the risk of a smaller consumer base in the future.
Over the past year, China has accelerated efforts to shore up its faltering birth rate by rolling out a series of family-support policies aimed at lowering child-rearing costs and easing the pressures that have deterred couples from having children.
Most notably, Beijing launched a national childcare subsidy scheme offering up to 10,800 yuan (US$1,534) per year for each child under three – the most far-reaching pronatalist measure since China allowed families to have three children in 2021.
In the early stages of population decline, fluctuations [in births] are common before the trend stabilises
Yuan Xin, China Population Association
Yuan Xin, vice-president of the China Population Association and a professor of demography at Nankai University in Tianjin, pointed to a correlation between the decline in births last year and the sharp drop in marriages in 2024, noting that marriage registrations typically serve as a leading indicator of birth trends in the following year.
China’s mainland registered 6.106 million marriages in 2024, down more than 20 per cent year on year, marking the lowest level since 1980. However, through the first three quarters of 2025, China recorded a year-on-year uptick of 8.5 per cent in marriage registrations, with analysts signalling that a host of pro-marriage policies were starting to pay off.
And while the nation’s full-year marriage figures have yet to be released, several major municipalities have reported sharp increases in registrations. Shanghai recorded a 38.7 per cent year-on-year rise in marriages in 2025, while Fujian province said the number of newly registered couples grew by 12 per cent.
Yuan, with the China Population Association, projected that the total number of marriages reached around 6.9 million last year, and he expected this to push births in 2026 to above 8 million.
“In the early stages of population decline, fluctuations [in births] are common before the trend stabilises,” he said. “But despite short-term volatility, a return to positive growth is almost off the table.”
Late last year, central authorities also pledged to expand insurance coverage to cover all childbirth-related out-of-pocket expenses, and they have sought to regulate childcare by addressing long-standing shortages, uneven quality and weak oversight in the sector.
At the same time, the government has moved to make it easier to register for marriage and harder to get a divorce, as shrinking marriage rates delay family formation and compound population ageing.
As the number of women of childbearing age shrinks, fertility intentions remain weak, and marriage and childbirth continue to be delayed, much stronger support is required at both national and local levels, according to experts.

data.title: Hackers target Iran state TV with exiled prince’s message to forces
data.description:
Hackers disrupted Iranian state television satellite transmissions to air footage supporting the country’s exiled crown prince and calling on security forces to not “point your weapons at the people”, footage online showed early Monday, the latest disruption to follow nationwide protests in the country.
The hacking came as the death toll in a crackdown by authorities that smothered the demonstrations reached at least 3,919 people killed, activists said. They fear the number will grow far higher as information leaks out of a country still gripped by the government’s decision to shut down the internet.
Meanwhile, tensions remain high between the United States and Iran over the crackdown after US President Donald Trump drew two red lines for the Islamic Republic - the killing of peaceful protesters and Tehran conducting mass executions in the wake of the demonstrations.
The USS Abraham Lincoln aircraft carrier, which days earlier had been in the South China Sea, passed Singapore overnight to enter the Strait of Malacca - putting it on a route that could bring it to the Middle East. Tracking data showed that the USS Frank E. Petersen Jr., the USS Michael Murphy and the USS Spruance, all Arleigh Burke-class guided missile destroyers, were travelling with the Lincoln through the strait.

The footage aired Sunday night across multiple channels broadcast by satellite from Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting, the country’s state broadcaster which has a monopoly on television and radio broadcasting.
The video aired two clips of exiled Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi, then included footage of security forces and others in what appeared to be Iranian police uniforms. It claimed without offering evidence others had “laid down their weapons and swore an oath of allegiance to the people”.
“This is a message to the army and security forces,” one graphic read. “Don’t point your weapons at the people. Join the nation for the freedom of Iran.”
The semi-official Fars news agency, believed to be close to the country’s paramilitary Revolutionary Guard, quoted a statement from the state broadcaster acknowledging that the signal in “some areas of the country was momentarily disrupted by an unknown source”. It did not discuss what had been aired.
A statement from Pahlavi’s office acknowledged the disruption that showed the crown prince. It did not respond to questions from Associated Press about the hack.
“I have a special message for the military. You are the national army of Iran, not the Islamic Republic army,” Pahlavi said in the hacked broadcast. “You have a duty to protect your own lives. You don’t have much time left. Join the people as soon as possible.”

Social media footage shared abroad, possibly from those with Starlink satellites to get around the internet shutdown, showed the hack in progress across multiple channels. Pahlavi’s campaign also shared the footage.
Sunday’s hack is not the first to see Iranian airwaves disrupted. In 1986, The Washington Post reported that the CIA supplied the prince’s allies “a miniaturised television transmitter for an 11-minute clandestine broadcast” to Iran by Pahlavi that pirated the signal of two stations in the Islamic Republic.
In 2022, multiple channels aired footage showing leaders from the exiled opposition group Mujahedeen-e-Khalq and a graphic calling for the death of Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Pahlavi’s father, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, fled Iran ahead of the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Pahlavi, the son, urged protesters onto the streets on January 8 as Iranian authorities shut down the internet and drastically intensified their crackdown.
How much support Pahlavi has inside Iran remains an open question, though there have been pro-shah cries at the demonstrations.
The death toll exceeds that of any other round of protest or unrest in Iran in decades, and recalls the chaos surrounding the 1979 revolution. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency put the death toll Sunday to at least 3,919 people killed, warning it likely would go higher.
Iranian officials have not given a clear death toll, although on Saturday, Khamenei said the protests had left “several thousand” people dead and blamed the United States for the deaths. It was the first indication from an Iranian leader of the extent of the casualties from the wave of protests that began December 28 over Iran’s ailing economy.

data.title: What an ancient Chinese hero’s fatal love of raw fish warns us about uncooked food
data.description:
Tokyo’s famed New Year fish auction has smashed its own headline record, with a giant bluefin tuna fetching an eye-watering price. At the Toyosu fish market’s first auction of 2026, a 243kg (535lb) bluefin tuna was snapped up for 510 million yen (US$3.2 million), making it the most expensive tuna ever sold at this annual ritual.
The winning bid came from Kiyomura, the company behind the popular Sushi Zanmai chain. The purchase eclipsed the company’s own record-setting bid from 2019.
The fish itself was caught off Oma in northern Japan, a region famed for producing premium sushi-grade tuna that reliably sends bidders into a frenzy. As always, the pre-dawn spectacle drew crowds and cameras.
Beyond bragging rights for Kiyomura, this year’s splurge reflects a cautiously optimistic trend: bluefin stocks are slowly recovering, thanks to conservation measures after years of overfishing. That has made these record-breaking sales feel a little less controversial than they once did.

Raw seafood is – or used to be – one of my favourite foods. I have long fancied myself something of a connoisseur of Japanese sashimi and sushi, preferring the lighter, subtler flavours of lean tuna, sea bream and squid to the bolder, oilier notes of mackerel and salmon, the latter being my least favourite fish when eaten raw.
I get triggered when I see diners drowning slices of sashimi in soy sauce, or worse, stirring wasabi-in-a-tube – usually horseradish with food colouring and assorted additives – into the soy sauce to produce a disgusting green sludge that bears an unfortunate resemblance to the pea soup chucked up by the demon-possessed girl in The Exorcist.
In the past year or so, however, I have drastically cut back on uncooked seafood and meat. It is not just sashimi and sushi that have fallen by the wayside, but also former indulgences such as raw oysters on the half shell, steak tartare and Korean yukhoe. I no longer dare order a medium-rare hamburger, and I will only eat salads that I have prepared myself.
This retreat has nothing to do with woo-woo notions about raw food having bad qi that causes stomach upsets, an article of faith for my mother and many others of her generation. It has everything to do with a growing reluctance to ingest parasites and pathogens that are not neutralised by heat.
Food safety authorities have warned for years that uncooked seafood and meat are high-risk foods, no matter how fresh, expensive or expertly prepared. Most of us, myself included, simply chose to ignore them.

I pay heed now, probably because of the cowardice and instinct for self-preservation that come with age, coupled with the dawning realisation that my five-decade-old immune system is no longer what it once was.
History offers a sobering cautionary tale. Chen Deng (163-201), celebrated for his heroism, loyalty and learning, died because of his love for raw fish.
An able district administrator who improved the lives of the people under his charge, Chen was also a capable military commander.
He fell gravely ill one day, and the famed physician Hua Tuo (140-208) correctly diagnosed a parasitic infection due to the over-consumption of raw fish. He prescribed a herbal concoction that caused Chen to throw up three sheng – about 600 millilitres, or 20 fluid ounces – of red, wriggling worms. It is an image worth recalling the next time one is tempted to eat something uncooked.
Chen, alas, died not long after, at the tender age of 38.
These days, my appetite for raw delicacies is tempered by health concerns and the vivid mental picture of Hua Tuo’s unfortunate patient. Some delicacies, I have decided, are simply not worth the gut ache. Or worse.
